bio.notes: Replacement naming after first Hooker Norton (H00399) died Sep 29, 1742
bio.extra_names: Called 'No. 2' in EH 1909 because his elder brother Hooker Norton (H00399, b. 1741) had received the same name — the elder Hooker presumably died young and the parents reused the name in 1744.

narrative_blocks.header: Given his deceased elder brother's name
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Norton was the SECOND son of Reuben and Hannah Hooker Norton to be given the name Hooker. The first (, b. 1741) presumably died young. Three years later when this son was born in 1744 the parents reused the Hooker name — a common eighteenth-century replacement-naming pattern that EH itself notes by labeling him 'No. 2.'

narrative_blocks.header: Eleven children five died young
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker and Sibyl Bradley Norton had eleven documented children. Five died in infancy or childhood: Daniel Hooker at nine months in 1764; Russell at age six in 1774; Hannah at age eight in 1787; a second Russell at age eleven in 1795; and Zenas at age nine that same year. The 1795 deaths of Russell and Zenas in the same year hint at an epidemic.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived eighty-three years through three wars
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Norton was born in colonial Connecticut on January 15, 1744 — age eleven at the start of the French and Indian War, age thirty-two when Independence was declared. He outlived the Revolutionary War, the War of 1812, and his wife Sibyl by twenty-one years. He died July 17, 1827 in Madison CT at eighty-three.

Three years later when this son was born in 1744 the parents reused the Hooker name — a common eighteenth-century replacement-naming pattern that EH itself notes by labeling him 'No. 2.'

He outlived the Revolutionary War, the War of 1812, and his wife Sibyl by twenty-one years. He died July 17, 1827 in Madison CT at eighty-three.

parents.father_research_notes: Joseph Bradley of Vergennes VT. Per EH 1909 entry 401.
parents.mother_research_notes: Sibyl Meigs Bradley of Vergennes VT. Per EH 1909 entry 401.

narrative_blocks.header: Family migrated to Vermont before her death
narrative_blocks.body: Sibyl Bradley Norton was born in Guilford CT in 1745 and married Hooker Norton there in 1762 at seventeen. By her 1806 death the family had migrated to Sunderland, Bennington County, Vermont — establishing that the Hooker Norton household made the New England westward move sometime between 1786 (when her youngest son Zenas was born in CT) and 1806. Her husband Hooker returned to Madison CT to die in 1827.

narrative_blocks.header: Two sisters married Evarts brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Lorain Norton married Ezra Evarts of Guilford in 1783; nine years later her younger sister Lois married Ezra's brother Curtis. Both Evarts brothers were sons of Jonathan and Lucretia Parmalee Evarts. Lorain and Ezra removed to Vermont; Lois and Curtis stayed in Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Evarts siblings married three Nortons
narrative_blocks.body: Lorain Norton married Ezra Evarts in 1783. Years later Ezra's brother Curtis married Lorain's sister Lois, and their sister Mabel Evarts married Lorain's uncle Eber Norton. Three Evarts siblings married three Nortons across two generations — an unusually dense intermarriage between the two Guilford families.

parents.father_research_notes: Jonathan Evarts of Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1046.
parents.mother_research_notes: Lucretia Parmalee Evarts of Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1046.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Evarts siblings married three Nortons
narrative_blocks.body: Ezra Evarts married Lorain Norton in 1783. His brother Curtis later married Lorain's sister Lois, and their sister Mabel married Lorain's uncle Eber Norton. The three Evarts siblings married three Nortons across two generations of the same Guilford family.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Evarts siblings married three Nortons
narrative_blocks.body: Lois Norton married Curtis Evarts in 1792. Her sister Lorain had married Curtis's brother Ezra nine years before, and the Evartses' sister Mabel married Lois's uncle Eber Norton. Three Evarts siblings tied themselves to three Nortons — Guilford genealogy at its densest.

parents.father_research_notes: Jonathan Evarts of Guilford CT. Same parents as his brother Ezra (X02570).
parents.mother_research_notes: Lucretia Parmalee Evarts of Guilford CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Evarts siblings married three Nortons
narrative_blocks.body: Curtis Evarts married Lois Norton in 1792. His brother Ezra had married Lois's sister Lorain in 1783, and his sister Mabel had married Lois's uncle Eber Norton in 1788. Three Evarts siblings tied themselves to three Nortons across two generations of the same Guilford family.

burial.plot_notes: Hindes Cemetery, Addison, Addison County VT. FindAGrave indicates burial here despite EH-recorded death in Mexico NY 1845 — most likely his remains were returned to Addison alongside his first wife Lydia.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died three days after twins born
narrative_blocks.body: Bela Norton's first wife Lydia Bushnell gave birth to unnamed twins on March 17, 1813. She died three days later on March 20 — strongly suggesting complications from the birth. Bela went on to marry Mary Paine and then Mary Ann Mulliken.

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated west to Mexico New York
narrative_blocks.body: Bela Norton was born in East Guilford CT in 1772 but ended his life two thousand miles away — in Mexico, Oswego County, New York, where he died in June 1845 at age seventy-two. The migration date is not documented; he had spent his early married years in Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated four states across his lifetime
narrative_blocks.body: Bela Norton's life traced the New England westward migration in four stages: born in Connecticut in 1772; married Lydia Bushnell in Cheshire, Massachusetts in 1794; raised his children in Addison, Vermont where Lydia died 1813; ultimately died in Mexico, Oswego County, New York in 1845. His remains appear to have been returned to Vermont — buried Hindes Cemetery, Addison.

burial.plot_notes: Hindes Cemetery, Addison, Addison County VT. FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Saybrook bride who died on the Vermont frontier
narrative_blocks.body: Lydia Bushnell was born in Saybrook on the Connecticut shore in November 1772 and married Bela Norton at age twenty-one in Cheshire MA. They raised at least twelve children in Vermont, including unnamed twins born March 17, 1813. Lydia died three days after the twins' birth — almost certainly from childbirth complications — at age forty in Addison VT.

bio.extra_names: Maiden surname not preserved in EH; previously widow of Mr. Mulliken

narrative_blocks.body: Diantha Amelia Norton's birth year is given as 1796 by Edward Hooker in 1909 but as 1795 by her FindAGrave memorial. April 10, 1795 is preferred. She married Henry H.

narrative_blocks.header: Two brothers married two Hill sisters
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Norton married Damaris Hill of East Guilford in the 1790s. Years later his younger brother Daniel married Damaris's sister Lois Hill. The two Norton brothers raised their twenty-one combined children with two Hill sisters as wives — a sibling-double marriage that doubled the cousin relationships in the next generation.

parents.father_research_notes: James Hill of East Guilford CT. Father of both Damaris (Benjamin Norton's wife) and Lois (Daniel Norton's wife). Per EH 1909 entries 1051 and 1054.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mabel Blatchley Hill of East Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entries 1051 and 1054.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her paternal grandmother Sibyl Bradley Norton.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two teenage daughters in a year
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Norton and Lois Hill Norton lost their daughters Catherine (age fifteen) and Eliza (age thirteen) within nine months of each other in 1835-1836. Catherine died April 1835; Eliza followed January 1836. The cause is not documented but the close timing suggests an epidemic illness.

parents.father_research_notes: James Hill of East Guilford CT. Same parents as Damaris Hill (X02575).
parents.mother_research_notes: Mabel Blatchley Hill of East Guilford CT.

narrative_blocks.body: Catherine died in 1835 at the age of fifteen.

narrative_blocks.body: Eliza died in 1836 at the age of thirteen.

notable.notable_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate, 1703
notable.primary_url_label: Historic Buildings CT — Dr. Daniel Hooker House 1725

narrative_blocks.header: First Yale tutor, B.A. graduate in 1703
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first tutor appointed at the Collegiate School (Yale) by first rector Abraham Pierson Jr. — Yale's earliest faculty member. He received his Yale B.A. in 1703, alongside Rev. John Hart who was the first student to graduate.

narrative_blocks.header: Surgeon of the Canada expedition, 1711
narrative_blocks.body: He served as surgeon of the Massachusetts expedition against Canada in 1711 — the final major military operation of Queen Anne's War. He then returned to Wethersfield to practice medicine and law.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined his father's pulpit — chose medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, Farmington invited Daniel to succeed his father. He declined the pulpit and chose medicine — studying law alongside it, not to practice but to help patients make their wills.

narrative_blocks.header: First Farmington-born man with a college education
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Harvard in 1700, then enrolled at the new Collegiate School in Saybrook to receive its B.A. in 1703. He was the first Farmington-born man to obtain a college education.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited and divided Thomas Hooker's West Hartford farm
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited Thomas Hooker's farm in West Hartford and in 1728 deeded it to his sons. The land had been part of the original Hooker grant — passing through three generations from founder to surgeon.

institutions.institution_blurb: First Yale tutor 1701-1704; first B.A. graduate 1703.

cross_connections.display_label: He tutored under Pierson — Yale's first rector.
His niece married John Hart, his Yale successor
His successor as Yale tutor — second graduate

career.notes: First tutor of Yale Collegiate School. Hired by rector Abraham Pierson Jr. (X02127). Resigned in favor of Rev. John Hart (I00137).
Settled Wethersfield after leaving Yale. Also studied law to help patients make wills — no evidence he practiced law professionally.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard 1700. First Farmington-born man to receive a college education.

Received degree at Yale's first commencement 1703. EH says he was "the first B.A. of Yale" but also that Rev. John Hart (I00137) was "nominally the second, but really the first actual student to graduate." Daniel was the tutor/faculty; Hart was the first student B.A. Conflict flagged — see research_notes.

bio.title: Doctor
bio.bio_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate; later a Wethersfield physician

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)

sources.notes: Wethersfield Village Cemetery; carved by William Stanclift

burial.plot_notes: Marker type: gravestone. Original marker survives. Carved by William Stanclift. One of the older surviving carved gravestones in the region.
